posts.name: Chris Tuttle
posts.cooked:
Not much chatter on here about this grape, but I think it deserves more consideration given all of the love for chardonnay, chenin blanc, and melon de bourgogne one finds on this board.

My experience is profoundly limited in number of producers, but Bachelet’s 2014 last night was bonkers from the first sniff. I love it when you put your nose in the glass and realize that a wine has a chance to deliver on multiple sensory vectors. So many times it’s difficult to coax out any kind of significant impression via smell alone (maybe that says something about my wine choices ).

Anyway, this wine tasted of tart lemons and limes and finished with salt (yes, not wussy saline) and plenty of acidity.

Not much chatter probably because not much is made and it’s rarely made into something to contemplate. It’s one of those grapes that people plant because they always did. And it’s blended with some creme de cassis for Kir, which isn’t the way to earn any respect. Kind of like trying to run for senate based on your career as a hooker, which might be a smart move in some quarters.

Anyway, I always doubt those judgements that declare a grape to be greater or lesser. I think it would be pretty interesting to treat that grape more seriously and see what happens. Apparently some producers in Burgundy are doing exactly that, so let’s see what comes of it. And I’m sure there are plenty of areas in which it could be pretty interesting - maybe New York for example? I don’t know of anyone in the US making it, but I’d like to try it if I found some.

Chris Tuttle:

My experience is profoundly limited in number of producers, but Bachelet’s 2014 last night was bonkers from the first sniff. I love it when you put your nose in the glass and realize that a wine has a chance to deliver on multiple sensory vectors. So many times it’s difficult to coax out any kind of significant impression via smell alone (maybe that says something about my wine choices ).

Anyway, this wine tasted of tart lemons and limes and finished with salt (yes, not wussy saline) and plenty of acidity.

Yep, I love the 2014 Bachelet Aligote. Bought a half case and already worked through half of it.

Very much reminds me of the 2014 Briords but with more weight and texture.

Lemonade for grownups (in a great way).

posts.name: Chris Tuttle
posts.cooked: How would an aligote like this example (plenty of tart fruit) change over time? Do you have any experience you could share?
I had the 2006 Ponsot Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Clos des Monts Luisants Vieilles Vignes back last November at Piazza Duomo in Alba and loved it…it’s a full-bodied vision of what Aligote can be though.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
I’ve had aligotes before, but I can’t say I remember any.

No, that’s being too kind. They were too neutral to care one way or another.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I haven’t had it in a while, but the Bouzeron Aligoté from Dom. de Villaine (owned by the family of Aubert de Villaine of DRC) can be wonderful – full-bodied but with lots of acid and true minerality.

Sadly, on non-pro WS, the Bachelet shows up only at some store in New Orleans. Has anyone seen it in the Northeast, and NY in particular?

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I have been and will always be a big proponent of Aligoté, the original white grape variety of Burgundy.
The Bachelet is consistently of the greatest examples produced.
At the moment, these are the in stock goodies (ws pro needed to see, John…)
2014 Denis Bachelet
2014 Dureuil Janthial
2015 Ch de la Maltroye
2011-2014 Michel Lafarge
2014 Bruno Clavelier
Each of these are terrific examples in their own right.
A salty santé to you !

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Bachelet’s version is very good. I like Ramonet and Villaine’s Bouzeron very much. Leroy’s Aligoté is off the charts. Ponsot’s Monts Luisants is stellar and there’s Aligoté in their Corton-Charlemagne. We import versions from Boisset, Fournier and Lucien Jacob, all excellent drinks.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked:
PYCM makes an excellent (and very PYCM) aligote. Usually not that hard to get. The most amazing one though is the d’auvenay sous le chatellet. A truly great, grand Cru level wine, but priced accordingly.

We did a wonderful dinner at the square a few years ago where everyone brought only Aligotes. We had pretty much all of them as I recall!

posts.name: Peter Papay
posts.cooked:
2015 Francois Mikulski Bourgogne Aligote
Very fresh and crisp, perfect for the summer. Got all the stuffings and I like where this is heading to.

As usual we are highly limited through the LCBO as far as the availability of various producers concerns. A quick search revealed 2 producers. SAQ on the other hand has over 16 to offer. There is slightly more selection via the consignment program but you have to commit to purchase a whole case.
if people in this thread havent heard it, the IDTT podcast on Aligote is worth a listen! Erin does a pretty good job on breaking it down.

posts.name: W. Leonard
posts.cooked:
Robert Panzer:

I have been and will always be a big proponent of Aligoté, the original white grape variety of Burgundy.
The Bachelet is consistently of the greatest examples produced.
At the moment, these are the in stock goodies (ws pro needed to see, John…)
2014 Denis Bachelet
2014 Dureuil Janthial
2015 Ch de la Maltroye
2011-2014 Michel Lafarge
2014 Bruno Clavelier
Each of these are terrific examples in their own right.
A salty santé to you !

Lafarge makes a great Aligoté! It also showcases how the pre-clonal Aligoté Doré can create serious wines opposed to Aligoté Vert which is a clonal selection for higher yields. Sylvain Pataille started bottling single parcels in 2013. I think we will be talking more and more about Aligoté in the coming years!!

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: There’s really D’Auvenay’s sous le chatelet and then everyone else and the gap is wider than in any other wine I know. Sadly, the price gap is equally wide.

posts.name: M Simon
posts.cooked:
Nolan E:

Young Inglewood is growing some Aligote in St Helena, I haven’t tried it but I’m curious.

Nolan is correct. We have approximately 250 Aligote vines at our estate in St. Helena, Napa Valley. The vines are only on their sixth leaf and are producing roughly one barrel a year at this point. As far as we know, we produce the only Aligote from Napa Valley. I have heard that there is a single row of it growing in Carneros at Lee Hudson’s vineyard and it was widely planted in this area prior to prohibition.

There is a group of Aligote producers in Burgundy called Les Aligoteurs. They had their first tasting this past April and we were delighted to be invited to join and pour our Napa version among the Burgundians. I believe the group has 20 something members at this point.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Chris Tuttle:

How would an aligote like this example (plenty of tart fruit) change over time? Do you have any experience you could share?

They tend to become more honeyed in terms of aromas and flavors, and more satiny on the palate, while retaining their incisiveness. Of course, some just die. ‘Tart fruit’ is a pretty catch-all way to describe the vast majority of Aligoté…

One new Aligoté producer from Bouzéron that should be on folks radars is Domaine Les Champs de Thémis. Already one of the best in the village (the competition is not overwhelming).

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Have worked my way through the best part of a case of PYCM’s 2016 version over the past couple of months. Simply delicious white Burgundy.

posts.name: Ray Ormand
posts.cooked: It is also grown in Bennett Valley. Ernest Vineyards bottles it under their Edaphos label. I found the 2016 remarkably tart. Takes some getting used to.

posts.name: Ian Fitzsimmons
posts.cooked:
Jay Miller:

I love the de Moor aligotes (especially the A Ligoter) and the Ponsot Luisants.

I’ve never tried the de Moor, but I bought some magnums of Ponsot 2004 dirt cheap some years ago. The Ponsot site used to feature fiches on these wines that advised not opening them before (something like) 12 years, and I opened one in 2016. My note from CT:

"This wine is a 20/80% Chardonnay-Aligote blend, per Ponsot - or was, until 2004, when it the Chardonnay vines were pulled out. Out of the bottle it is diamond dry, inviting oxidation apprehensioin; but with a bit of air time, you realize that the Aligoté is just damn tart with acidity. It evolves to a perfectly fresh, tart, intense, medium-bodied white, very clean on the palate, delightful to drink. Not a ton of fruit, a bit lemony, in keeping with the acidity theme. Not a great wine, but a good one with really decent finesse; and the freshness is striking.

This wine continued to improve with additional time open in the 'fridge until, finally, it reminded me more of an excellent Chitry - think, say, De Moor."

Probably time to open another magnum soon. Why don’t you come down to D.C. and visit the folk here?

I also recently had a 2015 Leroy (the negoce) and Coche-Dury. The Leroy had a ton of reduction & yeast, wasn’t enjoyable even on day two. The Coche was delicious but for retail $ probably not so much.
I also really like the Young Inglewood “Vin Clair” (Malbec Rose).

I’ve had some really good ones from Ente, Leroy, Roulot and Fichet.

I’m new to this game (previously used Jaffelin Aligote for Kir) but recently got a case of the Benoit Ente. Very interesting. My note on a preliminary bottle was “ Opens lean very dry. Acidic but not sharp. Takes 2-3 hours to fully open up. Go slow on these.” To Watch, as they say.

posts.name: A Songeur
posts.cooked:
You can get boring and superb aligote. In the later category, A Coche Dury aligote was miles better and more interesting than his Bourgogne Blanc. PYCM is also excellent as are Benoit Ente and Bart (“only often” but superb QPR) .
I will not mention below par examples. Still got to taste Arnaud Ente but quite hopeful as he usually performs better than Benoit.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Still got to taste Arnaud Ente but quite hopeful as he usually performs better than Benoit.

2018 was the last vintage made of Arnaud Ente’s Aligoté, as the vines were ripped up (though the “2019” did get a laudatory review from at least one journalist earlier this year). So if you want to taste it—and I agree you should—you’d better do it sooner rather than later.

Jadot bought their Bouzeron parcel from de Villaine, interestingly. It’s a nice plot.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked: Here in Oregon, we’re starting to see plantings. In particular, a new block at Temperance Hill Vineyard, Eola-Amity Hills. Check out the Lumos Aligoté (Lumos is the vineyard manager, Dai Crisp’s winery). It is excellent!

posts.name: Ashley Bair
posts.cooked:
Never had the Leroy, D’Auvenay, Coche-Dury, or Dujac Aligotés, but I opened a 2018 Anne Buisson Bourgogne Aligoté tonight, and it was as impressive as any Aligoté I’ve had outside the Ponsot Clos des Monts Luisants. Anne’s Aligoté comes from 60+ year old vines in Meursault, and hers has a lot more to offer than all but the very best village Meursaults, not to mention many 1er cru Meursaults. It’s superior to the de Villaine Bouzeron IMHO, which has always been my standard-bearer for readily available Aligoté.

Thus far, I have not had anything notable from the West Coast, but have not had those that have been mentioned in this thread, either. (As usual, it’s the result of me not traveling there, and therefore not joining any mailing lists.)

Nobody has mentioned Patrick Piuze yet, but his 2017 Bourgogne Aligoté 1953 was excellent a couple of years ago. Benoit Ente also made an excellent Bourgogne Aligoté in 2017.

Finally, to Dan’s comment, there is some very interesting Elbling being made these days if you enjoy mineral-driven, elegant, bone dry white wines. Check out Hild and Jonas Dostert in the Obermosel.

love Aligote!

there is some great examples out there from Oregon and Burgundy. my favorite is from Chantereves. they do a great job

posts.name: L. ZHU
posts.cooked:
the 2019 Charles Lachaux aligote is brilliant, but super hard to find
